speaker: interviewer
text: October 19, 1995, continuing with Marcel Baum. Recording by [? Eda ?] [? Gordon, ?] Westminster, California, USA. Tape number five.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were saying you were liberated and you got to Limoges. And did you, at that point, know the whereabouts of the rest of your family? And who was with you when you go to Limoges.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we-- we did not know who was still living from the rest of the family. The only thing I learned when-- once we got to Limoges, we went to the lady where-- who hid my sister first. Her name was Mrs. Roch, R-O-C-H. And she told us then that our parents were still living in Bonnac-la-Cote. And my mother usually came almost every day to Limoges to see if she could find us. But unfortunately, that day she didn't come. She didn't find us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we came to Madame Roch, she got us transportation and notified our parents that we were home, and that we both were there. So we went back to Bonnac-la-Cote. And then we learned that my sister was hidden in a convent for over a year. My parents were hidden in several farms because they were staying [INAUDIBLE] in one farm, then they have to be hidden in another farm. But we always-- they always find-- found nice French people who were hidden them, if it wasn't one or the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was always somebody who was taking care of my parents. So we were-- we are-- our family is very fortunate that we all five are survivors. Except-- except that later on the parents died, you know. And so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- I'm going back a little bit-- when was liberation day for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: June-- June 5, 1945. Never ever forget that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Never forget that. And when you got to Limoges, how long it took you to get to Limoges?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took us almost 10 days to get to Limoges because, you know, different trains, different transportation from where we were. It wasn't-- wasn't easy, you know. We just couldn't get a ticket and say we go there and there. And also there were a lot of destruction, you know, bombing in-- in Germany, and all that. So we couldn't get the train, too. So-- But it was-- it was a joyous day of my life to be home again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was your encounter with your mother? Tell me a little bit about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother treated us like children when we came home. And she did the right thing, because I see in many of my friends who were with us died after they were home a week or two. My-- my dad treated us [INAUDIBLE], mashed potatoes. She started us like children to eat, so that our stomach would start learning, [? absorb ?] food again. She didn't want to give us any fatty food, rich food, so that we wouldn't get sick off it. Or she wouldn't let us drink any alcohol or anything like this, because a lot of the younger people who came back with us to France, they came home. [INAUDIBLE] the family was so happy to see everybody, had big parties and things. And you eat-- they ate foods they shouldn't have eaten. And they became very ill. But our mother really treated us like she wanted to keep us. So that was really, I mean, thoughtful and-- and considerate. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you stay in Limoges a long time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we stayed in Bonnac-la-Cote for about another six or seven weeks. Then we went back to Vic-sur-Seille, our town where we came from. And we still were Germans at that time. And it was difficult to find jobs, because the French won the war against the Germans. So it was a very, very difficult as a German refugee to find work. But I always managed to find a-- a job as a baker. In our-- when I came back. I worked for a Mr. [? Jung ?] as a baker. And he took me in, paper or no paper. He gave me a job. My brother, on the other hand, had a much harder time to find a job. So he went to sell materials to farmers and-- and-- and stuff like this. And then, it was so difficult-- while it was so difficult to find job, we decided to immigrate to the United States. So we wrote to some of our family here in the United States in '45, '46. And we finally got the papers in 1940-- the end of '46. And we immigrated to the United States in April 5, 1947.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother and I. We were the first two. And then we started to get jobs and work. Our family didn't want to take everybody, because, you know, in those days, when you had to be responsible of immigrants. If-- you didn't get any money from the state if you were-- like-- like today people come in, they can collect social security or anything. In those days, you had to be independent. You had to make your own living, otherwise you couldn't come into the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we-- we came in 1947. I started to work in Chicago. And my brother started work as a butcher. I started as a baker. And I was making 1947-- actually, I do work hard-- $65 a week. And you know what that was, $65 a week in 1947? That's like $400-$500 today. In Chicago.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chicago. But I worked also from midnight to 12:00-- 11:00 or 12 o'clock the next day. And obviously are saying, the European, they're getting someplace. You know. Why can't we get some place? I didn't-- I wasn't afraid to work. And then we made the paper out for my sister and parents. And my parents came then in 1949.

speaker: interviewee
text: Alex is 15 months older than I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: Alice is 60-- six years younger than I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Six years younger than you are, yes. And in Chicago, did you have a lot of people you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. We had a lot of relatives. We had a lot of cousins in Chicago. And when my parents came, you know, in Chicago, you couldn't find apartments. That was the most difficult thing in Chicago when my parents came in '49 to find an apartment. We lived in one room apartment with the five of us again [LAUGHS] for about two months, three months. Then we finally found an-- an apartment in an attic, and we moved all into there. And naturally, I met my wife in 1947.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruth. We went-- in 1947 we came to Chicago. There was a club. And that was mostly German Jew-- Jewish kids who got together, mostly the Germans. And they had a dance. And my cousin said, you go with us to the dance, and started to go dance with my wife. And also one-- one day my wife called-- called-- up and said let's go to the movie because her dad worked for a cousin of mine who was in the meat business. So matching, you know, everything comes together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in-- we-- we went together for a long time. And one day, because I used to ask her, let's go out next week. Let's go out. So she asked me, can we go steady? I didn't know what steady meant. [LAUGHS] I laughed about it. And I said, uh-uh. I said, I never-- I do not get married until my folks are here, until Mom and Dad come. In 1949, my folks came in June-- in May or June. And in 1950, we got married. January 1, 1950, my wife and I got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife-- my mother lost all her three kids in one year. My brother got married March, three months later in March. And my sister got married in June that same year. So my mother lost all her three kids in one year. [LAUGHS] But we lived with my parents. Ruth and I stayed with my parents for almost eight years. We wouldn't leave-- leave them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names and their ages? If you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Morris is 44. Allan is 42. And Jamie is 35.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewer
text: Eight grandchildren. That's great. And how has the experience of the Holocaust changed you? Did it change you as a person?

speaker: interviewee
text: It sure does. I mean, we went a few weeks ago, you know, to the Hidden Children conference, we were there. It really puts back memories what you didn't think of, you know, what you tried to forget. And also, it brings memory that you cannot discuss with people who have not gone through the same [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] era that we did, being hidden, being in camps, being shipped from location to location. And that really makes a different person out of you. But like we went to Germany a year ago. They invited us to the inaugurate the-- the temple. And they were so nice to us. When we left, they asked us how we were feeling. So we told them, we can forgive. But one thing we cannot do is to forget. We never will forget what has happened. And-- and I hope this will not happen again, in this country, or-- or in any country, because you see what's going on now, again. You see the skinhead coming up. You see that [INAUDIBLE] coming up, hating the Jews again. I hope we-- I hope us Jews we don't have to go through a period like we did again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have a message that you'd like to leave for your children and your grandchildren in the world, and anything else you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: My message is, I hope my children, when they see this, that they will remember what their father went through. And hope that they will always remember. And see, too, that the world doesn't-- that this does not happen again in this world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to introduce your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Ruth. Say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we've been married now for almost 46 years. Married the 1st of January, 1950 so he wouldn't forget the date.

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name is Lorig, L-O-R-I-G.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in [INAUDIBLE] by Trier, near Luxembourg. It's all along the Moselle area. And I think we've had a pretty good marriage. We have a nice family. And we're pretty proud of the kids. So we-- I lived-- well, when we married we lived with Marcel's parents for the first eight years of our marriage. And so I got to hear all the family stories from my mother-in-law's side about what happened to her first husband, and what happened to the boys, you know, Marcel and Alex, and Alice. So I lived through all these stories for all these years.

speaker: interviewee
text: She actually knows the family better than I do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only because he didn't pay attention. But my mother-in-law was a very interesting woman. She was more or less of a spell binder storyteller. She could really bind the family together. And she did bind the family together. She was always-- her greatest wish was that her three children never have any dissension. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think her greatest fear was that once she and Father were gone, that they would no longer have contact with one another. But that's very untrue. They have a strong family relationship. We're always in contact with one another.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's been very interesting, very nice. We've gone back to Germany three times, right? Yeah. And twice I've visited my hometown where I still meet-- well, we met over a bottle of wine with a girl I went to grade school. And it's very funny, but her-- her aunt and uncle were friends of my mother and dad's in the United States, the Brown family. And so it's nice they have that contact. And when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came April 21, 1936. A long time ago. We were very fortunate. My grandmother's family furnished the proper affidavits for us, because in those days, you couldn't come over without any kind of a sponsor. And in order to ensure the welfare of the family, my jobs were always secured for people who came over, even if it wasn't their profession. My father was a butcher by trade. But when he came to America he learned to become a baker's helper by making frosting at a bakery. And then he got a job for, I think-- he told my mother-- called my mother up and he got a job for $25 a week. And that was a phenomenal amount of money in the late '30s. Because America was still under the thumb of the Depression. So-- but I think my sister and I had a pretty good upbringing. We had no problems. And during the Second World War, you didn't speak very much German outside the home, because it wasn't very well-accepted. I still have my enemy alien registration card that children and grownups who were from Germany or Italy had to carry with them. A long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to Los Angeles? What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: 1961. And what was your occupation here in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- then my occupation was being a delicatessen man. And from there, I became a supervisor for the delicatessen for the Ralphs Grocery Company. And I worked for them for almost 18 years. So I retired from Ralphs Grocery Company.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, you've got Chad, he's going to be 18 on the 27th of this month. And then we have Tony, he's 16. And Jason is 14. We have Joshua who is--

speaker: interviewee
text: 13, yes. And then Michael is six. And Daniel will be five. And Rebecca is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Three. I almost forgot my oldest little granddaughter, Loralie. She is all of 10.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I think we have a pretty nice family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she older than you or younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Been very nice having you here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, it was nice being here. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father and mother after six weeks of marriage. This is Moritz and Laure Baum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what year this was taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where? Do you know the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany. I don't know where.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this-- I'm sitting in the chair. And my-- next to it is my brother Alex. This is Alex and Marcel Baum. This was taken when my brother was about five-- three years old. And I was but a year and a half old.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Laufersweiler, I believe. I don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, again, Alex and Marcel Baum. This was taken in the backyard of our house. And we were about eight and seven years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is also in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And Alex is the tallest?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Alice, and me, Marcel. This was taken also in Germany. And this was in the year of 1934.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Baum, Marcel Baum. And her name is Alice Joseph. Marcel Baum-- oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Marcel Baum at the age of 13, right after my bar mitzvah. This was taken in Laufersweiler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- how old were you then?

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what-- year was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this is me, Marcel Baum. And this picture was taken in 1947 for a passport picture to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: In France. I don't know where. In [? North Sea, ?] I believe. I can't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother. Her new name-- her name was Laure Joseph. And this picture was a passport picture. And this was taken in [? North ?] [? Sea ?] in 1948.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my father and mother on a vacation in Switzerland in 1962. In 1962.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Marcel and Ruth Baum. And this was taken in 1990 here in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Morris Baum, his wife, Susan, his son, Jason, and his daughter, Loralie. This was taken at the bar mitzvah one year ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my middle boy, Allan Baum, with his wife, Ellen, and his older son, Chad, and his other son is Josh. And-- and next to it is Jason at their bar mitzvah in 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: This again is on the bar mtizvah, my old-- my youngest son, Jimmy, and his wife Tina, their son, Tony, Michael, Daniel, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in this one, we have to--

speaker: interviewer
text: We need to see where everyone is, so starting with the little girl on the left, who is that?

words.text: on the left, who is that?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and the little boy above Tina.

speaker: interviewee
text: Holding Daniel is Jason-- Jimmy Baum.

speaker: interviewee
text: And holding the candle is Tony--

speaker: interviewee
text: Belelo. No, that's another. Belelo is his name. And then next little boy is Michael Baum. And then Jason Baum, the bar mitzvah boy. It was taken in '94 here in California.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I see that. I see it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture on the boat trip to the Panama Canal. That's to the left is my brother, Alex Baum. In the center is my sister, Alice Hanau. And on the right, that's me, Marcel Baum. And that was taken in September of '93.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the trip to the Panama Canal.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the number I was assigned to-- to in-- in Buchenwald in 1943. '43. If you see, the little triangle has an F in it, and that's because I was prisoner on the-- on the French, not as a Jew. And that's why the triangle has an F in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does just a red triangle mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know what the triangle meant to the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a postcard I wrote in Buchenwald in 1943 to notify my family that we were still living. The postcard had to be written in Germany. The postcard was made to a family Senamaud who hid my mother and dad.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an identification card, what we had to carry on-- on-- on yourself at all time in France. You could not travel in France without any identification. And this one, in particular, notified the government that we were in camp [? Dora-- ?] or in Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where does it say Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: It doesn't say it on this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an identification card that I--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the identification card that I carried around in concentration camp to go from one department to another department in 1944, in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this s-- the [? fiche ?] is a transportation document, what we had to use when we were liberated out of concentration camp to go from city to city until we came home to Limoges. You can see the name of Limoges.
